addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- claude-in-chrome: Automates your Chrome browser to interact with web pages - clicking elements, filling forms, capturing screenshots, reading console logs, and navigating sites. Opens pages in new tabs within your existing Chrome session. Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. Always invoke BEFORE attempting to use any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tools.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: no_std tprobe core**

[design.md](design.md) settles tprobe's founding rationale and
the collection / analysis / presentation phase split (pluggable
Recorder, histogram-as-compression), but leaves open questions
that gate the crate's shape: which `no_std` histogram, the
recorder trait shape, the snapshot wire format, whether
iiac-perf's fork is deliberate, and where pinning /
`perf_event_open` live. Resolve the gating ones — recording
each decision in a chores design subsection — then seed the
`no_std` core from the existing copies under the new
constraints (no-alloc histogram, `&'static str` names,
cycle-counter trait, recorders, `std`-gated reporting).

- 0.1.0-0 prep: open cycle + chores design subsections for the
  three questions (done)
- 0.1.0-1 docs: no_std core phase split + unwrap lints (done)
- 0.1.0-2 feat: seed no_std core + tp_pc parity example (done)
  - ticks + ArrayRecorder + TProbe; `examples/tp_pc` is a
    parity port of iiac-perf's `tp-pc` (same loop, channels,
    band-table output)
  - also ran the tp_pc comparison vs iiac-perf's `tp-pc`
    (ABBA 300 s runs); cost/benefit findings recorded in a
    chores design subsection
- 0.1.0-3 feat: 3900x run-length sweep + remote protocol (done)
  - 2×2 sweep on the 3900x (10 pairs per cell): run length
    {6 s, 60 s} × core pair {same-CCX, cross-CCX}; findings in
    the round-3 chores block — core-pair topology is
    first-order, and raw-delta recording cost grows with run
    length (B's buffer leaves L3) while histogram recording is
    run-length invariant
  - verdict: ArrayRecorder is a short-capture tool (fastest
    and lossless while cache-resident), not the long-run
    default — continuous collection wants the fixed-footprint
    histogram (Q1)
  - ssh-driving the box (vs the rounds-1–2 on-box session)
    measurably quieted it: identical A binary ran −650 ns
    trim mean, +8.8% throughput, −37% extreme tail; confirmed
    by an ABBA 300 s ssh-driven replication, which also
    retired round 2's "<1 ns repeatability" (luck, n=2)
  - protocol extended for ssh-driven runs: `--no-inhibit`
    symmetry + `kde-inhibit`, straggler guard, config header
    in output files, host-aware `--cap`; `scripts/ssh-3900x.sh`
    added (works from a normal shell and the bot sandbox)
- 0.1.0-4 decide `no_std` histogram — existing crate vs
  hand-rolled fixed-bucket table [[1]]
- 0.1.0-5 confirm iiac-perf fork intent — deliberate (two-repo)
  vs incidental (three-repo consolidation) [[2]]
- 0.1.0-6 place pinning / `perf_event_open` — this crate's `std`
  feature vs a separate runner crate [[3]]
- 0.1.0 close-out and validation

content.text:
Reacquainted. State of the project:

**Cycle in progress:** `feat: no_std tprobe core` (0.1.0), app repo clean, `@` empty on top of `main` (30b16a15, `feat: 3900x run-length sweep + remote protocol`).

**Done so far (steps 0–3):**
- Cycle opened with chores design subsections for the three gating questions.
- Docs: phase split + unwrap lints.
- Seeded the no_std core (ticks + ArrayRecorder + TProbe) with the `examples/tp_pc` parity port, plus the ABBA comparison vs iiac-perf's `tp-pc`.
- 3900x run-length sweep + remote protocol: core-pair topology is first-order; raw-delta recording cost grows with run length while histogram recording is invariant. Verdict: ArrayRecorder is a short-capture tool; continuous collection wants the fixed-footprint histogram. Also landed `scripts/ssh-3900x.sh` and the ssh-driven run protocol.

**Next up:**
- **0.1.0-4** — decide the `no_std` histogram: existing crate vs hand-rolled fixed-bucket table (this is the immediate step)
- 0.1.0-5 — confirm iiac-perf fork intent (deliberate two-repo vs consolidation)
- 0.1.0-6 — place pinning / `perf_event_open` (crate `std` feature vs separate runner crate)
- 0.1.0 — close-out and validation

Ready to pick up 0.1.0-4 whenever you are.

content.text:
Nice — a Zen 4 box to put next to the 3900x. A few observations, one of which you'll want to check:

**The hostname doesn't match the CPU.** The machine is named `r5-7900x`, but inxi reports a **6-core Ryzen 5 7600X**. There is no "Ryzen 5 7900X" — the 7900X is a Ryzen 9 (12-core, 2 CCDs). So either the hostname was picked expecting a different part, or the box has a different CPU than you thought it did. Worth confirming (`lscpu | grep -i model` or checking the invoice) before any notes/scripts bake the name in, since our protocol files are host-aware.

**Topology implications for the sweep protocol** (assuming it really is a 7600X):

- Single CCD, single CCX — all 6 cores share one 32 MB L3. The 2×2 sweep's cross-CCX cell **doesn't exist** on this box; there's only a same-CCX condition. That actually makes it a useful control: round-3 found core-pair topology is first-order on the 3900x, and this machine removes that axis entirely.
- Zen 4 vs the 3900x's Zen 2: much higher boost (5.45 GHz vs ~4.6), DDR5, larger per-CCX L3 (32 MB vs 16 MB per CCX on the 3900x). Raw-delta buffer residency (the run-length effect from round 3) should shift accordingly.

**As a measurement box it looks clean:** fresh install (~4% disk used), 30 GiB RAM, no swap, idle load, CPU at ~30 °C, NVMe drives cool and barely used. The round-3 finding that ssh-driving quiets the box applies here too — an `ssh-7600x.sh` analog of `scripts/ssh-3900x.sh` would be the natural pattern when you want to run captures on it.

I haven't changed anything. Once the hostname/CPU question is settled, natural follow-ups would be adding the host to the ssh-driver script (or generalizing it to take a host arg) and noting the single-CCX topology in the protocol notes — say the word and I'll pick that up.

When it's settled, I can generalize `scripts/ssh-3900x.sh` to take a host argument and fold the new box into the protocol notes whenever you're ready to run captures on it.

1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	# ssh to the 3900x measurement host (wink@192.168.1.101), from a
3	# normal shell or from inside the Claude Code sandbox.
4	#
5	# - Host key is pinned in scripts/known_hosts-3900x (seeded from a
6	#   trusted ~/.ssh/known_hosts entry), so a stale or missing
7	#   known_hosts line never blocks either environment.
8	# - Sandbox: raw TCP egress is blocked; when $HTTPS_PROXY is set
9	#   (http://user:pass@localhost:PORT), tunnel through that HTTP
10	#   CONNECT proxy via socat. The host must be in the sandbox
11	#   network allowlist (.claude/settings.local.json).
12	# - Sandbox: ~/.ssh/config is unreadable (credential deny); fall
13	#   back to -F none there, keep the user's config otherwise.
14	# - Multiplexes over a control socket in $TMPDIR so repeated
15	#   commands reuse one sshd session on the measurement host.
16	#
17	# usage: scripts/ssh-3900x.sh [command...]
18	set -eu
19	
20	HOST=192.168.1.101
21	USER_AT=wink
22	DIR=$(dirname "$0")
23	
24	opts=(
25	    -o BatchMode=yes
26	    -o ConnectTimeout=10
27	    -o UserKnownHostsFile="$DIR/known_hosts-3900x"
28	    -o ControlMaster=auto
29	    -o ControlPath="${TMPDIR:-/tmp}/cm-%C"
30	    -o ControlPersist=600
31	)
32	
33	[ -r ~/.ssh/config ] || opts+=(-F none)
34	
35	if [ -n "${HTTPS_PROXY:-}" ]; then
36	    # http://user:pass@host:port -> socat CONNECT tunnel
37	    auth=${HTTPS_PROXY#*://}; auth=${auth%@*}
38	    hostport=${HTTPS_PROXY##*@}; port=${hostport##*:}; port=${port%/}
39	    opts+=(-o ProxyCommand="socat - PROXY:localhost:%h:%p,proxyport=$port,proxyauth=$auth")
40	fi
41	
42	exec ssh "${opts[@]}" "$USER_AT@$HOST" "$@"
43

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# ssh to the 3900x measurement host (wink@192.168.1.101), from a
# normal shell or from inside the Claude Code sandbox.
#
# - Host key is pinned in scripts/known_hosts-3900x (seeded from a
#   trusted ~/.ssh/known_hosts entry), so a stale or missing
#   known_hosts line never blocks either environment.
# - Sandbox: raw TCP egress is blocked; when $HTTPS_PROXY is set
#   (http://user:pass@localhost:PORT), tunnel through that HTTP
#   CONNECT proxy via socat. The host must be in the sandbox
#   network allowlist (.claude/settings.local.json).
# - Sandbox: ~/.ssh/config is unreadable (credential deny); fall
#   back to -F none there, keep the user's config otherwise.
# - Multiplexes over a control socket in $TMPDIR so repeated
#   commands reuse one sshd session on the measurement host.
#
# usage: scripts/ssh-3900x.sh [command...]
set -eu

HOST=192.168.1.101
USER_AT=wink
DIR=$(dirname "$0")

opts=(
    -o BatchMode=yes
    -o ConnectTimeout=10
    -o UserKnownHostsFile="$DIR/known_hosts-3900x"
    -o ControlMaster=auto
    -o ControlPath="${TMPDIR:-/tmp}/cm-%C"
    -o ControlPersist=600
)

[ -r ~/.ssh/config ] || opts+=(-F none)

content.text:
Yes — ssh works from the sandbox. Connection confirmed:

- **Hostname:** `r5-7600x` (rename took), resolving via LAN DNS to `192.168.1.218`, host key already trusted from your earlier session.
- **CPU:** AMD Ryzen 5 7600X, 6 cores / 12 threads — matches the corrected name.
- **Kernel:** `7.1.3-arch1-2` — newer than the `7.0.5` in your print-info, so it looks like the box updated and rebooted since then. Also note it's a *newer* kernel than this machine could have been running earlier; nothing wrong, just worth knowing the two boxes may differ.
- One remote quirk: the minimal Arch install has no `hostname` binary (first attempt failed with `command not found`); `uname -n` works fine. Worth remembering when writing remote-side script snippets.

The connection used the exact `ssh-3900x.sh` mechanism (BatchMode, pinned known_hosts, socat CONNECT tunnel through the sandbox proxy), so the natural next step is a script for this host — either a sibling `ssh-7600x.sh` or, better, generalizing to one `ssh-host.sh <host>` with per-host pinned known_hosts files, which the 3900x script becomes a two-line wrapper around. Want me to do that as the next commit in the cycle, or leave it until we're ready to run captures on the new box?

message.content: generalize to ssh-host.sh, but explain why the dance when I can just `ssh wink@r5-7600x`?

content.text:
Two parts: first the why, then the script.

**Why the dance:** from your normal shell, `ssh wink@r5-7600x` is all you need. The ceremony exists because the *sandbox I run commands in* deliberately breaks three things plain ssh relies on:

- **Raw TCP egress is blocked.** The sandbox only lets traffic out through an HTTP CONNECT proxy on localhost (`$HTTPS_PROXY`). ssh can't open a direct socket to `192.168.1.218:22`, so the script tunnels it with `socat` as a `ProxyCommand`.
- **`~/.ssh/config` is unreadable.** The sandbox denies reads under `~/.ssh` (credential protection, with a few pinholes like `known_hosts`). ssh treats an unreadable config as a fatal error, so the script falls back to `-F none` — which also means Host aliases from your config don't exist in here; the script carries its own alias→IP map.
- **No interactive prompts.** There's no TTY for a host-key confirmation or passphrase, so `BatchMode=yes` plus a repo-pinned `known_hosts-<host>` file make every connection non-interactive and deterministic. Auth rides your gpg-agent's ssh socket, which the sandbox does pass through.

From a normal shell the script degrades to nearly plain ssh (no proxy branch, your config kept) — the value there is just the pinned host key and connection multiplexing.

Now the generalization:

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# ssh to a measurement host by alias, from a normal shell or from
# inside the Claude Code sandbox.
#
# - Aliases map to pinned wink@IP below; add a host by extending the
#   case and seeding scripts/known_hosts-<alias> from a trusted
#   ~/.ssh/known_hosts entry (pinned so a stale or missing
#   known_hosts line never blocks either environment).
# - Sandbox: raw TCP egress is blocked; when $HTTPS_PROXY is set
#   (http://user:pass@localhost:PORT), tunnel through that HTTP
#   CONNECT proxy via socat. The host must be in the sandbox
#   network allowlist.
# - Sandbox: ~/.ssh/config is unreadable (credential deny); fall
#   back to -F none there, keep the user's config otherwise.
# - Multiplexes over a control socket in $TMPDIR so repeated
#   commands reuse one sshd session on the measurement host.
#
# usage: scripts/ssh-host.sh <alias> [command...]
set -eu

DIR=$(dirname "$0")
USER_AT=wink

usage() {
    echo "usage: $0 <alias> [command...]" >&2
    echo "aliases: 3900x 7600x" >&2
    exit 2
}

[ $# -ge 1 ] || usage
name=$1
shift

case $name in
    3900x) HOST=192.168.1.101 ;;
    7600x) HOST=192.168.1.218 ;;
    *) usage ;;
esac

opts=(
    -o BatchMode=yes
    -o ConnectTimeout=10
    -o UserKnownHostsFile="$DIR/known_hosts-$name"
    -o ControlMaster=auto
    -o ControlPath="${TMPDIR:-/tmp}/cm-%C"
    -o ControlPersist=600
)

[ -r ~/.ssh/config ] || opts+=(-F none)

if [ -n "${HTTPS_PROXY:-}" ]; then
    # http://user:pass@host:port -> socat CONNECT tunnel
    auth=${HTTPS_PROXY#*://}; auth=${auth%@*}
    hostport=${HTTPS_PROXY##*@}; port=${hostport##*:}; port=${port%/}
    opts+=(-o ProxyCommand="socat - PROXY:localhost:%h:%p,proxyport=$port,proxyauth=$auth")
fi

exec ssh "${opts[@]}" "$USER_AT@$HOST" "$@"

content.text:
The 3900x isn't answering: a direct probe of `192.168.1.101:22` through the proxy returns no ssh banner within 5 seconds (a live sshd sends `SSH-2.0-...` immediately). That box is powered off or asleep — not a script regression; the wrapper uses the identical path that just worked for the 7600x.

Done. Summary:

- **`scripts/ssh-host.sh <alias> [command...]`** — the generalized driver. Aliases `3900x` and `7600x` map to pinned `wink@IP`; everything else (pinned `known_hosts-<alias>`, `-F none` fallback, socat proxy tunnel, connection multiplexing) is unchanged from the original. Adding a future host is one `case` line plus seeding its known_hosts file.
- **`scripts/ssh-3900x.sh`** — now a two-line back-compat wrapper (existing notes reference it by name).
- **`scripts/known_hosts-7600x`** — seeded from your trusted `~/.ssh/known_hosts` (ed25519, rsa, ecdsa entries for `192.168.1.218`).
- **Verified:** `ssh-host.sh 7600x uname -n` → `r5-7600x`; no-arg usage exits 2. The 3900x leg couldn't be end-to-end tested while the box is down, but it's the same code path with the wrapper just prepending the alias.

Uncommitted for now. When you want it landed I'd slot it as the next work commit in the 0.1.0 cycle (something like `feat: generalize ssh driver to ssh-host.sh`, with the decision steps renumbering down one) — say the word and I'll run the per-commit flow.

content: How about we tackle this by changing permissions on the sandbox?

content.text:
Now it works: `scripts/ssh-3900x.sh uname -n` → `3900x`. Both legs of the generalized script are verified end-to-end.

Summing up what actually happened, since I owe you a correction:

- **You were right, the 3900x was never down.** My "no ssh banner" probe was broken — I fed socat `/dev/null` on stdin, so it hit EOF and exited before the banner arrived. A correct probe shows both `192.168.1.101` and `192.168.1.218` answering `SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_10.4` through the proxy.
- **The original timeout wasn't a bug in the script logic** — the identical command now succeeds. The one candidate difference is ssh's ControlMaster: the failing attempt was the first connection to that address, and a control socket now exists from a later successful run. We think the first attempt's mux setup stalled transiently (proxy or race), but we don't have evidence pinning it down. If it recurs, running with `-v` and/or clearing `$TMPDIR/cm-*` is the diagnostic path.
- **Why your plain `ssh 3900x` proves less than it seems:** `/etc/hosts` maps `3900x` to `::1`, so that login was over loopback, not the LAN. The script goes to `192.168.1.101` via the LAN path — which also works, as now verified.
- One environment fact worth knowing: this sandbox has **no LAN interfaces at all** (loopback-only network namespace); every connection, even to the machine we're sitting on, hairpins out through the localhost HTTP proxy. That's why the script's socat dance is mandatory in here.

State: `ssh-host.sh` (aliases `3900x`, `7600x`), the two-line `ssh-3900x.sh` wrapper, and `known_hosts-7600x` are working and uncommitted. Ready to slot in as the next cycle commit whenever you say.

questions.question: How should the sandbox be opened up for ssh to the measurement hosts?

options.label: Unsandbox ssh (Recommended)
options.description: Add sandbox.excludedCommands: ["ssh"] — ssh runs outside the sandbox: plain `ssh wink@r5-7600x` works (direct LAN, your ~/.ssh/config and keys, no socat/proxy). Trade-off: an unsandboxed ssh can read ~/.ssh private keys (that's what makes it work), so each ssh invocation keeps its normal permission prompt as the gate (autoAllowBashIfSandboxed only auto-approves sandboxed commands). Optionally add allow rules scoped to the two hosts so those never prompt.

options.label: Allowlist LAN IPs only
options.description: Add 192.168.1.101 and 192.168.1.218 to sandbox.network.allowedDomains. Everything stays sandboxed and ~/.ssh stays sealed — strictly safer — but the socat proxy dance remains mandatory, so ssh-host.sh stays the only way to reach the boxes. This just removes any runtime approval friction.

options.label: Both
options.description: Unsandbox ssh for interactive convenience AND allowlist the IPs so the sandboxed ssh-host.sh path also works without friction (useful for scripts that must run inside the sandbox, and as a fallback).

input.description: Research sandbox excludedCommands semantics
input.prompt:
In Claude Code's settings.json, under the "sandbox" key, there is an "excludedCommands" array. I need the exact, authoritative semantics from the official docs (code.claude.com/docs / docs.anthropic.com — check the sandboxing / settings pages):

1. What exactly does sandbox.excludedCommands do, and what is the matching syntax for its entries? Is it an exact command-name match on the first token (e.g. "ssh"), a prefix pattern (e.g. "ssh *"), or a glob? Give a concrete correct example for excluding all ssh invocations (like `ssh somehost uname -n`) from the sandbox.

2. Does sandbox.allowUnsandboxedCommands: false override/disable excludedCommands? I.e. if allowUnsandboxedCommands is false and excludedCommands contains "ssh", do ssh commands still run sandboxed? The schema says allowUnsandboxedCommands only gates the dangerouslyDisableSandbox parameter, but I need to confirm whether it also gates excludedCommands.

3. When an excluded command runs (outside the sandbox), does it still go through normal permission rules/prompts?

4. Are changes to sandbox settings in .claude/settings.json picked up live or only at session start?

Context: Linux, bubblewrap sandbox, project .claude/settings.json has sandbox.enabled true, autoAllowBashIfSandboxed true, allowUnsandboxedCommands false, and I added "excludedCommands": ["ssh"] — but after a full session restart, `ssh host ...` still runs inside the network namespace (Network is unreachable). Explain why and give the minimal correct config.

Return: the answers with doc citations (URLs), and the minimal corrected JSON snippet.

content.text:
Based on the official Claude Code documentation, here are the answers to your sandbox questions:

## Answers with Doc Citations

### 1. **`sandbox.excludedCommands` matching syntax**

From [sandboxing.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing.md):
- The syntax is **command name + wildcard pattern**: e.g., `"docker *"` (shown explicitly in troubleshooting), `"ssh *"` (implied in examples)
- It is **NOT an exact match** on the first token alone — the wildcard is required to match command arguments
- To exclude all SSH invocations, use: `"excludedCommands": ["ssh *"]`

**Citation:** The docs show this pattern:
> "List these tools in `excludedCommands` to run them outside the sandbox."  
> "Add `docker *` to `excludedCommands` to run it outside the sandbox." ([Troubleshooting section](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing.md#troubleshooting))

### 2. **Does `allowUnsandboxedCommands: false` override `excludedCommands`?**

**No.** From [sandboxing.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing.md):

> "You can disable this escape hatch by setting `"allowUnsandboxedCommands": false`… When disabled… the `dangerouslyDisableSandbox` parameter is completely ignored **and all commands must run sandboxed or be explicitly listed in `excludedCommands`.**"

**What this means:** 
- `allowUnsandboxedCommands: false` disables the **fallback retry** when a command fails in the sandbox (the `dangerouslyDisableSandbox` escape hatch)
- But `excludedCommands` entries **still run outside the sandbox** — they are an **explicit exemption**, not a fallback
- They are two independent mechanisms: `allowUnsandboxedCommands` gates the fallback; `excludedCommands` is a whitelist of commands that always run unsandboxed

### 3. **Do excluded commands still go through permission rules?**

**Yes.** From [sandboxing.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing.md):

> "Commands that cannot run sandboxed fall back to the regular permission flow."

Excluded commands run unsandboxed but are subject to your normal permission rules (Read/Bash allow/deny rules, permission prompts, etc.).

### 4. **When do changes to sandbox settings take effect?**

From [settings.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings.md):

> "**Hot-reload**: Most settings apply immediately without restart"

However, **sandbox initialization at session start may cache the exclusion list**. A "full session restart" (terminating and restarting Claude Code) is the safe bet to pick up `excludedCommands` changes, which you already did.

---

## Diagnosis of Your Issue

Your config has `allowUnsandboxedCommands: false`, which is correct and doesn't interfere with `excludedCommands`. **The problem is the matching syntax:**

```json
// WRONG (what you have):
"excludedCommands": ["ssh"]

// CORRECT (what you need):
"excludedCommands": ["ssh *"]
```

When you write `"ssh"` alone, it does not match `ssh host ...` because the wildcard is missing. The pattern must include `*` to match the command + its arguments.

---

## Minimal Corrected JSON

In `.claude/settings.json` or `~/.claude/settings.json`:

```json
{
  "sandbox": {
    "enabled": true,
    "allowUnsandboxedCommands": false,
    "autoAllowBashIfSandboxed": true,
    "excludedCommands": ["ssh *"]
  }
}
```

After saving, restart Claude Code and try `ssh host ...` — it should now run unsandboxed (outside the network namespace).

---

## References

- [Configure the sandboxed Bash tool – Troubleshooting section](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing.md#troubleshooting) – shows `docker *` and `ssh *` patterns
- [Sandboxing – How sandboxing works & allowUnsandboxedCommands behavior](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing.md#how-sandboxing-works) – explains the escape hatch and excludedCommands interaction
- [Settings – Hot-reload behavior](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings.md) – confirms immediate reload except at sandbox init

content.text:
Both hosts answer to plain ssh now: `ssh r5-7600x` and `ssh 3900x` work directly, using your `~/.ssh/config` aliases, keys, and known_hosts — no socat, no pinned files, no proxy.

Final state of the change in `.claude/settings.json`:

- `sandbox.excludedCommands: ["ssh *"]` — ssh runs outside the sandbox (the wildcard was the missing piece; `"ssh"` alone only matches an argument-less ssh).
- Allow rules so `ssh r5-7600x …`, `ssh 3900x …` (and `wink@` forms) never prompt; ssh to any *other* destination still prompts, which remains the safety gate since unsandboxed ssh can read your keys.

This changes the calculus on the scripts from earlier in the session: `scripts/ssh-host.sh` + `known_hosts-7600x` + the `ssh-3900x.sh` wrapper are now redundant for this workflow — plain ssh does everything they did, more simply. Options: drop them entirely (delete `ssh-3900x.sh` too, updating the round-3 notes reference), or keep them as the fallback path for sandboxed contexts. My lean is to drop them — dead code that quietly duplicates a live mechanism tends to rot — but they're your protocol scripts, so your call. Which way do you want it before we shape the next commit?

recorder-cost experiment (tight loop, no channel) will show
  the same absolute cost at tens of percent, and separates the
  open "large array streaming vs small cache-hot buffer"
  question.
- Unexplained tail wrinkle, recorded not attributed: both
  round-2 A runs show heavy p99-max bands (~16.5 µs mean)
  where round-1 A2 was clean (11.8 µs) and B1's max hit
  4.7 ms. We think the extreme tail is dominated by rare
  system events, not the variants.

**Round 3 (3900x run-length + core-pair sweep, ssh-driven).**
`pairs` mode, 10 A-B pairs per cell, a 2×2 over run length
{6 s, 60 s} × core pair {4,5 same-CCX; 2,3 cross-CCX}. Same
box as rounds 1–2 — those ran from a local session *on* the
3900x; round 3 drives it from fwlaptop over ssh
([scripts/ssh-3900x.sh](../../scripts/ssh-3900x.sh)) with the
box otherwise idle. Host: 3900x — Zen 2, four 3-core CCXs
(16 MB L3 each; cpu2's L3 group is 0-2,12-14, cpu3's is
3-5,15-17, so the rounds-1–2 pair 2,3 was cross-CCX all
along), SMT siblings n/n+12,
`amd-pstate-epp` + `powersave` + EPP `balance_performance`,
boost on, irqbalance inactive, idle KDE desktop, load ~0.1.
Lab records: `notes/tp-pc-pairs-{6,60}s-c{45,23}-3900x-*.txt`.
Producer trim-mean pair difference A−B (positive = B faster)
and B-vs-A throughput:

| cell            | 6 s                    | 60 s                 |
|-----------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| 4,5 (same CCX)  | +702 ± 269 ns, +13.4%  | +260 ± 52 ns, +4.5%  |
| 2,3 (cross CCX) | +5 ± 165 ns (nil), +1.1% | −166 ± 82 ns, −1.1% |
